Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I couldn't afford to stick to a perfect schedule because I was dealing with so much outside of school - money stress, family obligations, you name it. I'd find pockets of time, sometimes late at night or early morning, and just cram in as much listening and reviewing as I could. I learn best by hearing things, so I'd record lectures when I could attend or get audio from classmates and listen whenever I had a chance - on the bus, while doing chores, wherever.
Q2: My pattern was completely dependent on what crisis was happening that week. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything academic because life was just too overwhelming. Then when deadlines approached or exams were coming, I'd go into this intense mode where I'd study for hours straight. It wasn't healthy, but it's what worked given my circumstances. Regular schedules are a luxury I couldn't really afford.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or needing to check if there was something urgent I'd missed. I didn't log in casually to browse around - every login had a purpose because my time was so limited. Sometimes a classmate from my peer support group would message me saying there was something important posted, and that would prompt me to check. The LMS felt more like a necessary tool than something I wanted to engage with regularly.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became routine for me - it was always reactive. I'd go through phases where I'd try to check in more regularly, but then something would happen in my life and I'd fall off again. The only consistent thing was that I knew I'd eventually need to go there before assignments were due. My peers helped remind me, which was the closest thing to a routine I had.
Q5: The lecture recordings and any audio content were lifesavers for me. Since I'm an auditory learner, being able to listen to explanations multiple times really helped concepts stick. The discussion forums were also useful because I could see how other students understood things, and my peer support connections there kept me going. Video content was good too when I had the time to actually sit and watch it properly.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and PDF documents. With my low resources, printing wasn't an option, and reading long texts on a screen just doesn't work for my brain. I'd skim them at best or ask a peer to summarize the key points for me. Some of the optional practice quizzes I skipped too because I was always prioritizing what was graded and urgent over what might be helpful long-term.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something that would be assessed or if it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. I couldn't afford to spend time on things that felt like busywork. If I could tell that engaging with something would help me pass the exam or complete an assignment well, I'd make time for it no matter what else was going on.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Like, I knew they might help, but when you're stretched thin and anxious about everything, optional means 'skip it.' Required activities had clear consequences, so they got my attention. The optional stuff always felt like something I'd do 'when I have time,' but that time never really came. I sometimes felt guilty about skipping them, but survival mode doesn't leave room for extras.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download audio files and listen to them repeatedly without being connected. I'd borrow textbooks from the library or from friends and study from those. A lot of my learning came from study groups with peers where we'd discuss concepts out loud - that verbal processing really helped me. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be at a friend's place going over material together for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. Or I'd have downloaded a lecture early in the week and listened to it five or six times while doing other things - the system only saw one download. My best studying happened when I was talking through problems with my peer support group, but that was all face-to-face or through WhatsApp, not on the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd grab everything I needed in one session - download the audio, save any important documents - and then work with those materials for days without going back online. Data costs money, and I couldn't afford to be streaming or constantly connected. One download had to last me, so I'd make the most of whatever I pulled down during those brief login sessions.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engagement felt impossible. When my anxiety was really bad, I couldn't focus on anything - I'd just sit there feeling overwhelmed. Financial stress hit hard a few times during the semester, and I had to pick up extra work which meant school took a backseat. My attendance dropped because sometimes I literally couldn't get to campus, but I kept pushing through somehow.
Q13: Constantly. The workload felt crushing when combined with everything else in my life. Fatigue was a big one - I was often exhausted from working and worrying, which made it hard to engage meaningfully with anything. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because logging in just reminded me of how behind I was. My competing responsibilities definitely shaped when and how I could engage.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My mind would be somewhere else - worrying about bills, family stuff, whatever crisis was current. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed any of it. Those sessions felt wasted, but I was too anxious and exhausted to do better in those moments.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot even though I knew participating mattered. The anxiety of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge was real, even though I eventually did engage. I'd also avoid starting big assignments until the pressure became unbearable because the thought of beginning something major when I was already overwhelmed felt impossible. Avoidance was a coping mechanism, even if it made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all some weeks. I'd watch the due dates and work backward, cramming everything into the final days or hours before something was due. It was stressful and my anxiety would spike, but that pressure was the only thing that could cut through everything else competing for my attention.
Q17: Feedback was actually really motivating when I got it. Seeing that I was doing well on assignments despite everything gave me hope and kept me going. When I got good scores, it reminded me that I could do this even with all my struggles. The peer support feedback was especially meaningful - knowing others were rooting for me made a difference during the hardest times.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear deadlines gave me targets to aim for, which I needed. But the expectation of regular attendance and consistent engagement didn't account for students like me whose lives are unpredictable. I appreciated that there were multiple ways to access content, but the structure assumed a stability I didn't have.
Q19: Having recorded lectures made engagement possible for me - without those, I would have failed given my attendance issues. The peer interaction components were great because my support group became essential to my survival in the course. What made it harder was when things required consistent weekly participation or assumed everyone had reliable internet access and time. Flexibility helped; rigidity hurt.
Q20: I think those students might be desperately trying to figure things out but struggling to produce work. Maybe they're anxious like me and spend hours looking at materials without being able to start the actual assignments. Or they could be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to complete things even when they're technically present. High activity doesn't mean productive activity when you're in crisis mode.
Q21: That's literally my experience. My LMS activity was low, but I got a distinction because I found ways to learn that didn't show up in the system. I studied offline, worked with peers outside the platform, and focused my limited online time on exactly what I needed. The logs don't capture someone listening to a downloaded lecture ten times or having a three-hour study session with friends. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - like when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they're connected. That's useful for spotting students who might be disengaging entirely. But logs miss the depth of learning, the offline work, the peer support happening outside the system, and the personal circumstances affecting engagement. They measure presence, not understanding. They count clicks, not comprehension.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in genuine effort to understand it, even when it's hard. For me, it meant finding ways to keep going despite my anxiety and life circumstances. It's not about how many times you log in - it's about whether you're mentally present and working toward mastery. I was deeply engaged with this course even when the data might suggest otherwise.
Q24: The LMS can't show the mental and emotional battle I fought every single day just to keep going. It can't show the anxiety attacks before logging in or the relief when I finally understood something. It doesn't capture my peer support group meeting at someone's house to study together, or me listening to lectures while working my job. My resilience, my determination to succeed despite everything - none of that shows up in any log.
